title: Get started
text: SEMs ubiquitous social sciences, psychology, ecology, fields. INLAvaan package (Jamil Rue 2026b) provides user-friendly interface fitting Bayesian SEMs using Integrated Nested Laplace Approximations (INLA, Rue et al. 2009), based bespoke approximate Bayesian inference framework SEMs (Jamil Rue 2026a). vignette guide basics using INLAvaan fit simple SEM. begin make sure installed INLAvaan package GitHub running commands .
code: # Load all libraries for this example library(INLAvaan) library(tidyverse) library(lavaan)

title: Get started
text: motivate use SEMs, consider introductory example Song Lee (2012): poorer glycemic control lead greater severity kidney disease? observe three indicators glycemic control (y1y_1, y2y_2, y3y_3) three indicators kidney disease severity (y4y_4, y5y_5, y6y_6). Rather fitting separate regression models indicator, SEM allows us model relationship latent constructs , providing clearer coherent representation underlying processes. hypothesised SEM illustrated figure :

title: Get started
text: two-factor SEM described , easy simulate data using lavaan package . code : code , note true values parameters, including factor loadings Λ\Lambda, regression coefficient β\beta two latent variables, well residual latent variances Θ\Theta Ψ\Psi respectively.

title: Get started
text: Now simulated data, can fit SEM using INLAvaan. model syntax similar lavaan, making easy specify model. details model syntax, refer lavaan website. INLAvaan provides mirror functions main model fitting functions lavaan: acfa() mirrors lavaan::cfa() confirmatory factor analysis; asem() mirrors lavaan::sem() structural equation models; agrowth() mirrors lavaan::growth() latent growth curve models. code fit SEM model : INLAvaan computes approximation posterior density way Laplace approximation (Tierney et al. 1989; Jamil Rue 2026a). joint mode Hessian needs computed, gives Gaussian distribution joint posterior parameters. default method optimisation stats::nlminb(), optimisers can used specifying optim_method = "ucminf" ucminf package optim_method = "optim" call stats::optim() function method "BFGS". , marginal posterior distributions parameter can obtained one several ways, including 1) Skew normal fitting (marginal_method = "skewnorm", default method, see Chiuchiolo et al. 2023); 2) Two-piece asymmetric Gaussian fitting (marginal_method = "asymgaus", see Martins et al. 2013); 3) Direct marginalisation joint Gaussian posterior (marginal_method = "marggaus"); 4) Sampling joint Gaussian posterior (marginal_method = "sampling"). marginal posterior distributions obtained, can use compute derived quantities interest via copula sampling. posterior predictive p-values (Gelman et al. 1996) Deviance Information Criterion (DIC, Spiegelhalter et al. 2002) computed way. Often, posterior sampling takes longer model fitting , number samples can controlled via nsamp argument (default nsamp = 1000) can skipped altoghether (test = "none").

[189ms] #>  #> ℹ Computing the Hessian. #> ✔ Computing the Hessian. [38ms] #>  #> ℹ Performing VB correction. #> ✔ VB correction; mean |δ| = 0.057σ. [64ms] #>  #> ⠙ Posterior sampling and summarising. #> ✔ Posterior sampling and summarising. [312ms] #>

title: Get started
text: resulting object class INLAvaan, subclass lavaan objects. result, methods work lavaan objects also work INLAvaan objects. common ones probably coef() summary(). ’s possible request posterior medians modes summary output specifying postmedian = TRUE postmode = TRUE summary() function. Predicted values latent variables can obtained using predict() function. done sampling posterior distributions latent variables given observed data. function also supports predictions observed variables (e.g. type = "ov") missing data imputation, respecting multilevel structure present. S3 object summary method provides posterior means credible intervals latent variables. Alternatively, user welcome perform summary statistics list posterior samples returned predict(). diagnostics() function reports convergence approximation-quality metrics fitted model. Global diagnostics (type = "global") check whether optimiser converged, quantify well skew-normal marginals match joint posterior (via KL divergence NMAD). Per-parameter diagnostics (type = "param") provide gradient norms KL contributions free parameter, useful identifying problematic parameters. timing() function reports long computation stage took, can help identify bottlenecks scaling larger models. simple plot method provided view marginal posterior distributions parameters. vertical lines indicate posterior mode.

title: Get started
text: INLAvaan package uses prior specification syntax blavaan (Merkle Rosseel 2018; Merkle et al. 2021), detailed . Essentially, two ways set priors model parameters: 1) Globally parameters certain type (e.g., factor loadings, regression coefficients, etc.); 2) Individually specific parameters model syntax. default global priors derived blavaan: Note , INLAvaan uses separation strategy variance matrices, consequently places priors correlations instead covariances. , instead wished set global priors, say gamma distribution variances instead standard deviations (default), following: set individual priors specific parameters, can model syntax . instance, set normal prior mean 1 standard deviation 3 factor loading y3 eta1, normal prior mean 0 standard deviation 0.5 regression coefficient eta1 eta2, specify model follows:
code: priors_for()  # similar to blavaan::dpriors() #>                nu             alpha            lambda              beta  #>    "normal(0,32)"    "normal(0,10)"    "normal(0,10)"    "normal(0,10)"  #>             theta               psi               rho               tau  #> "gamma(1,.5)[sd]" "gamma(1,.5)[sd]"       "beta(1,1)"   "normal(0,1.5)" DP <- priors_for(theta = "gamma(1,1)", psi = "gamma(1,1)") DP #>              nu           alpha          lambda            beta           theta  #>  "normal(0,32)"  "normal(0,10)"  "normal(0,10)"  "normal(0,10)"    "gamma(1,1)"  #>             psi             rho             tau  #>    "gamma(1,1)"     "beta(1,1)" "normal(0,1.5)" ## fit <- asem(mod, dat, dpriors = DP)  # not run mod <- "   eta1 =~ y1 + y2 + prior('normal(1,3)')*y3   eta2 =~ y4 + y5 + y6   eta2 ~ prior('normal(0,.5)')*eta1 " ## fit <- asem(mod, dat)  # not run

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: Classical SEM fit indices (RMSEA, CFI, TLI, etc.) computed single maximum-likelihood chi-square statistic. Bayesian SEM, parameters random, posterior draw yields different chi-square value , therefore, different set fit indices. INLAvaan provides Bayesian analogues common indices, following framework Garnier-Villarreal Jorgensen (2020) (implemented blavaan). vignette covers: Mathematical definitions Bayesian fit indices. two rescaling methods ("devM" "MCMC"). worked example Holzinger–Swineford data. Comparison baseline model (incremental indices). Differences INLAvaan blavaan.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: Let 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} denote ss-th posterior draw model parameters (s=1,…,Ss = 1, \dots, S). per-sample deviance chi-square χs2=2[ℓsat−ℓ(𝛉(s))],   \chi^2_s = 2 \bigl[\ell_{\text{sat}} - \ell(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)})\bigr],  ℓsat\ell_{\text{sat}} log-likelihood saturated model (sample moments equal model moments) ℓ(𝛉(s))\ell(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)}) log-likelihood evaluated ss-th draw. equals NFML(𝛉(s))N \, F_{\text{ML}}(\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)}), FMLF_{\text{ML}} ML discrepancy function. INLAvaan supports two rescaling methods, controlled rescale argument: "devM" (default). Uses DIC-based effective number parameters pDp_D. ds=χs2−pD,df=p−pD,Nadj=N.   d_s = \chi^2_s - p_D, \qquad   \mathrm{df} = p - p_D, \qquad   N_{\mathrm{adj}} = N.  pDp_D unreasonable (pD≤0p_D \leq 0 pD≥pp_D \geq p), INLAvaan falls back using pD=qp_D = q (number free parameters). "MCMC". Uses classical chi-square N−1N - 1 scaling. ds=N−1Nχs2,df=p−q,Nadj=N−G,   d_s = \frac{N - 1}{N} \chi^2_s, \qquad   \mathrm{df} = p - q, \qquad   N_{\mathrm{adj}} = N - G,  qq number free parameters GG number groups. cases per-sample noncentrality parameter λ̂s=max⁡(ds−df,0)\hat\lambda_s = \max(d_s - \mathrm{df},\, 0). following indices computed posterior draw ss, generating posterior distribution. BRMSEA (Bayesian RMSEA). BRMSEAs=λ̂sdf⋅Nadj⋅G.   \text{BRMSEA}_s = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\lambda_s}{\mathrm{df} \cdot N_\text{adj}}} \cdot \sqrt{G}. BGammaHat. BGammaHats=vv+2λ̂s/Nadj,   \text{BGammaHat}_s = \frac{v}{v + 2\hat\lambda_s / N_\text{adj}},  v=∑gpgv = \sum_g p_g total number observed variables across groups. Adjusted BGammaHat. adjBGammaHats=1−pdf(1−BGammaHats).   \text{adjBGammaHat}_s = 1 - \frac{p}{\mathrm{df}} \bigl(1 - \text{BGammaHat}_s\bigr). BMc (McDonald’s centrality index). BMcs=exp⁡(−12λ̂s/Nadj).   \text{BMc}_s = \exp\!\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\hat\lambda_s / N_\text{adj}\bigr). Incremental indices compare target model baseline (null) model. Let ds(0)d_s^{(0)}, df(0)\mathrm{df}^{(0)}, λ̂s(0)\hat\lambda_s^{(0)} denote corresponding quantities baseline model. BCFI (Bayesian CFI). BCFIs=1−λ̂sλ̂s(0).   \text{BCFI}_s = 1 - \frac{\hat\lambda_s}{\hat\lambda_s^{(0)}}. BTLI (Bayesian TLI). BTLIs=ds(0)/df(0)−ds/dfds(0)/df(0)−1.   \text{BTLI}_s = \frac{d_s^{(0)} / \mathrm{df}^{(0)} - d_s / \mathrm{df}}{d_s^{(0)} / \mathrm{df}^{(0)} - 1}. BNFI (Bayesian NFI). BNFIs=ds(0)−dsds(0).   \text{BNFI}_s = \frac{d_s^{(0)} - d_s}{d_s^{(0)}}. posterior expectations (EAP), standard deviations, quantile-based credible intervals, modes distributions reported summary() method.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: fit three-factor CFA Holzinger–Swineford (1939) data. fitMeasures() returns posterior mean (EAP) Bayesian fit index alongside marginal log-likelihood, ppp, DIC, gradient diagnostics. inspect full posterior distribution index, use bfit_indices(). returns S3 object class "bfit_indices". text: rescale argument controls chi-square degrees freedom computed. default "devM", subtracts DIC-based pDp_D deviance. use classical N−1N - 1 scaling instead, set rescale = "MCMC": two methods generally produce different results, especially informative priors pDp_D deviates substantially qq.

title: Bayesian Fit Indices
text: INLAvaan’s Bayesian fit indices follow mathematical framework blavaan (Merkle et al. 2021), implementation differences: Currently, INLAvaan supports pDp_D DIC (.e., p_dic). "ppmc" rescaling method (computes replicated data posterior predictive) yet available.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: well model predict new data? Leave-one-(LOO) cross-validation answers holding one unit time, refitting model remaining data, scoring held-unit refitted posterior. total score expected log predictive density, elpdloo=∑u=1nlog⁡p(yu∣y−u),   \mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{loo}} = \sum_{u=1}^n \log p(y_u \mid y_{-u}),  rewards models predict well automatically penalises overfitting – making natural criterion comparing models. Computed naively, LOO needs nn refits. MCMC-based packages blavaan avoid importance-sampling posterior draws (Vehtari et al. 2017), still requires full set MCMC draws. INLAvaan instead exploits Laplace machinery: loo() approximates case-deletion posterior Taylor expansion around full-data posterior summary, entire LOO computed single fit – refitting sampling. Holzinger–Swineford example takes fraction second. Two unit types scored, resolved automatically model: LOSO (leave-one-subject-): single-level models, one unit per row. LOCO (leave-one-cluster-): two-level models, one unit per cluster – relevant predictive question “well model predict new cluster?”.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: Write ℓu(θ)=log⁡p(yu∣θ)\ell_u(\theta) = \log p(y_u \mid \theta) unit uu’s log-likelihood contribution, score sus_u Hessian HuH_u evaluated posterior summary (θ*,Σ)(\theta^*, \Sigma) fit. loo() reports first- second-order Taylor approximations log conditional predictive ordinate: log⁡CPOu(1)=ℓu−12su⊤Σsu,log⁡CPOu(2)=ℓu−12su⊤(Σ−1+Hu)−1su+12log⁡|+ΣHu|. \begin{aligned}   \log \mathrm{CPO}_u^{(1)} &= \ell_u - \tfrac12 s_u^\top \Sigma\, s_u, \\   \log \mathrm{CPO}_u^{(2)} &= \ell_u     - \tfrac12 s_u^\top (\Sigma^{-1} + H_u)^{-1} s_u     + \tfrac12 \log \lvert + \Sigma H_u \rvert . \end{aligned}  headline elpd_loo sum second-order terms, standard error nvar̂(log⁡CPOu)\sqrt{n \, \widehat{\mathrm{var}}(\log \mathrm{CPO}_u)}, looic =−2elpdloo= -2\,\mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{loo}} familiar information-criterion scale. effective number parameters ploo=∑u(lpdu−log⁡CPOu)p_{\mathrm{loo}} = \sum_u (\mathrm{lpd}_u - \log \mathrm{CPO}_u) uses analogous expansion full-posterior pointwise predictive density lpdu\mathrm{lpd}_u. rare unit whose second-order curvature matrix positive definite falls back first order (flagged output); warning raised happens many units, since suggests Gaussian posterior summary poor.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: fit classic three-factor CFA Holzinger–Swineford data. Fitting meanstructure = TRUE recommended LOO: otherwise unit log-likelihoods evaluated zero means absolute ELPD values biased (comparisons models data remain valid either way). pointwise contributions available inspection – useful spotting influential observations (large score_norm unusually low log_cpo_2 flags unit model predicts poorly):

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: ELPD differences models fitted data paired, standard errors computed pointwise differences rather marginal SEs. compare(..., loo = TRUE) automatically: Models sorted descending ELPD; elpd_diff se_diff relative best model. common heuristic difference smaller couple se_diff units practically meaningful (Vehtari et al. 2017) – three-factor model preferred wide margin.
code: one.factor <- "g =~ x1 + x2 + x3 + x4 + x5 + x6 + x7 + x8 + x9" fit1f <- acfa(one.factor, HolzingerSwineford1939, meanstructure = TRUE,               verbose = FALSE)  compare(fit, fit1f, loo = TRUE) #> Bayesian Model Comparison (INLAvaan) #> Models ordered by ELPD (Taylor LOO) #> elpd_diff/se_diff are paired differences vs the best model #>  #>  Model npar Marg.Loglik    logBF      DIC     pD      ELPD     SE  p_loo #>    fit   30   -3885.211    0.000 7534.755 29.370 -3769.109 42.945 32.433 #>  fit1f   27   -3990.563 -105.352 7757.068 26.932 -3878.134 46.800 27.516 #>  elpd_diff se_diff #>      0.000   0.000 #>   -109.025  17.072

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: models fitted cluster argument, loo() automatically switches per-cluster scoring (LOCO). covariates modelled jointly (fixed.x = FALSE); next section explains happens lavaan’s default covariate treatment.
code: model2l <- "   level: 1     fw =~ y1 + y2 + y3     fw ~ x1 + x2 + x3   level: 2     fb =~ y1 + y2 + y3     fb ~ w1 + w2 " fit2l <- asem(model2l, Demo.twolevel, cluster = "cluster",               meanstructure = TRUE, fixed.x = FALSE, verbose = FALSE)  loo(fit2l) #> Taylor leave-one-cluster-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 200 clusters (second-order Taylor approximation) #>  #>          Estimate     SE #> elpd_loo -23344.2  731.4 #> p_loo        34.3    2.0 #> looic     46688.3 1462.9

what: Exogenous covariates: joint and conditional scores
title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: model contains exogenous covariates, flavour LOO score follows likelihood model fitted . fixed.x = FALSE covariates receive saturated Gaussian block unit scored joint predictive density outcomes covariates (“surprised model brand-new unit, characteristics included?”). fixed.x = TRUE – lavaan’s default – fitted likelihood conditional one, unit scored predictive density outcomes given covariates (“given new unit known characteristics, well predict outcomes?”), familiar regression cross-validation convention. refitting trickery involved: conditional likelihood exactly invariant fixed covariate moments, conditional score carries additional approximation. two flavours estimate different quantities whose scales differ covariate predictive density, joint conditional elpd must never appear comparison – compare(..., loo = TRUE) refuses mixed-flavour comparisons outright. Within conditional flavour, however, models conditioning different covariate sets directly comparable long outcome variables match, makes covariate selection straightforward: (joint flavour comparison require retaining grade models, since joint scores models spanning different variable sets live different sample spaces.) flavours support covariate placement, including cluster-level within-level covariates two-level models.
code: model_x <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9   visual ~ ageyr + grade   textual ~ ageyr + grade " dat_x <- na.omit(   HolzingerSwineford1939[, c(paste0("x", 1:9), "ageyr", "grade")] )  # lavaan's default fixed.x = TRUE: scored conditionally on the covariates fit_cond <- asem(model_x, dat_x, meanstructure = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) loo(fit_cond) #> Taylor leave-one-subject-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 300 subjects (second-order Taylor approximation) #> Scored conditionally on the exogenous covariates (fixed.x fit) #>  #>          Estimate   SE #> elpd_loo  -3748.1 44.7 #> p_loo        45.1  2.7 #> looic      7496.2 89.5 model_x1 <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9   visual ~ ageyr " fit_cond1 <- asem(model_x1, dat_x, meanstructure = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)  compare(fit_cond, fit_cond1, loo = TRUE) #> Bayesian Model Comparison (INLAvaan) #> Models ordered by ELPD (Taylor LOO) #> elpd_diff/se_diff are paired differences vs the best model #>  #>      Model npar Marg.Loglik   logBF      DIC     pD      ELPD     SE  p_loo #>   fit_cond   32   -3875.892   0.000 7540.241 60.471 -3748.090 44.737 45.076 #>  fit_cond1   29   -3903.093 -27.201 7568.680 30.294 -3787.678 43.881 38.272 #>  elpd_diff se_diff #>      0.000   0.000 #>    -39.588  10.261

what: Storing the result with the fit
title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: loo(fit) never modifies fitted object – default test = "standard", fit already computes stores full LOO WAIC whenever model supported, mean structure, (LOO) predicted serial cost within 10-second budget. prediction calibrated run time timing single score evaluation, typical single-level models – full LOO costs fraction fit – simply get loo(fit) waic(fit) free. WAIC reuses draws fit produced posterior summaries, costs one casewise pass. expensive cases can force LOO regardless budget fit time, store afterwards explicit reassignment: stored result returned instantly loo(fit) reused fitMeasures() (blavaan-style names appear) compare(..., loo = TRUE):
code: fit <- acfa(HS.model, HolzingerSwineford1939, meanstructure = TRUE,             test = c("standard", "loo")) fit <- add_loo(fit) fitMeasures(fit, c("elpd_loo", "se_loo", "p_loo", "looic")) #>  elpd_loo     p_loo     looic    se_loo  #> -3769.109    32.433  7538.217    85.890

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: widely applicable information criterion (Watanabe 2010) asymptotically equivalent LOO also available. Unlike loo(), waic() sampling-based: evaluates unit log-likelihoods posterior draws, results carry Monte Carlo error, units pwaic,u>0.4p_{\mathrm{waic},u} > 0.4 trigger reliability warning – case prefer loo() (Vehtari et al. 2017).
code: set.seed(1) waic(fit) #> WAIC (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 1000 posterior draws and 301 subjects #>  #>           Estimate   SE #> elpd_waic  -3769.1 42.9 #> p_waic        32.0  2.1 #> waic        7538.2 85.9 #>  #> 10 units with p_waic > 0.4: the WAIC may be unreliable; prefer loo().

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: theta Sigma arguments evaluate LOO arbitrary Gaussian posterior summary instead fit’s . Combined Gaussian conditioning, scores constrained submodel encompassing fit alone. example, score submodel visual ~~ speed covariance fixed zero, condition summary parameter re-evaluate: refit took place: conditioned summary covariance locked zero (row column Sigma_c vanish), LOO machinery automatically restricts remaining parameters. pair arguments building block custom model-search strategies – screen many candidate restrictions conditioning, score loo(), refit winners. INLAvaan deliberately provides just evaluation API; search logic design.
code: int <- get_inlavaan_internal(fit) theta <- int$theta_star Sigma <- int$Sigma_theta  p <- which(names(coef(fit)) == "visual~~speed") theta_c <- theta - Sigma[, p] * (theta[p] / Sigma[p, p]) Sigma_c <- Sigma - tcrossprod(Sigma[, p]) / Sigma[p, p]  loo(fit, theta = theta_c, Sigma = Sigma_c) #> Taylor leave-one-subject-out cross-validation (INLAvaan) #> Computed from 301 subjects (second-order Taylor approximation) #>  #>          Estimate   SE #> elpd_loo  -3785.9 45.0 #> p_loo        34.0  2.5 #> looic      7571.9 90.1 #>  #> Evaluated at a user-supplied (theta, Sigma) summary.

title: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation
text: Supported models. Single-group, complete-data, continuous-indicator models fitted ML estimator. Multigroup, missing-data, ordinal (PML) models supported yet. Models exogenous covariates scored jointly (fixed.x = FALSE) conditionally (fixed.x = TRUE), following fitted likelihood, covariate placement. Parallelism opt-. default runs serially; pass loo(fit, cores = 2) parallelise Hessian stage via forking (available Windows). First order vs second order. second-order correction matters: validation tracks brute-force refits within one ELPD unit, first-order score can tens units. Use second_order = FALSE quick screening. Row deletion two-level models. loo(fit2l, type = "loso") forces per-row scoring two-level fit (contribution conditional density row given rest cluster). provided diagnostic, warns used, expensive large datasets – consider units argument subset.

title: Mediation Analysis
text: verify INLAvaan recovers correct parameters, simulate data “truth” known. logic follows: Generate… XX normally; MM dependent XX coefficient 0.5; YY dependent MM coefficient 0.7. Critically, add XX generation YY. means true direct effect (cc) 0, relationship fully mediated. expect model estimate ≈0.5a \approx 0.5, b≈0.7b \approx 0.7, indirect effect ab≈0.35ab \approx 0.35. direct effect cc close zero.
code: set.seed(11) n <- 100  # sample size  # 1. Predictor X <- rnorm(n)  # 2. Mediator (Path a = 0.5) M <- 0.5 * X + rnorm(n)  # 3. Outcome (Path b = 0.7, Path c = 0) Y <- 0.7 * M + rnorm(n)   dat <- data.frame(X = X, Y = Y, M = M)

title: Mediation Analysis
text: standard lavaan syntax mediation model straightforward (note use := operator define indirect effect new parameter.): model fit using asem(). meanstructure = TRUE argument supplied estimate intercepts variables. user may wish specify different prior distributions parameters. See relevant section Get started vignetted details.
code: mod <- "   # Direct effect (path c)   Y ~ c*X    # Mediator paths (path a and b)   M ~ a*X   Y ~ b*M    # Define Indirect effect (a*b)   ab := a*b    # Define Total effect   total := c + (a*b) " library(INLAvaan) fit <- asem(mod, dat, meanstructure = TRUE) #> ℹ Finding posterior mode. #> ✔ Finding posterior mode. Looking Regressions Defined Parameters sections output: intercepts non-significant, since simulated data true means zero. Path aa (M ~ X) estimated 0.525 (true value 0.5). Path bb (Y ~ M) estimated 0.771 (true value 0.7). Path cc (Y ~ X) estimated -0.060. 95% Credible Interval [-0.291, 0.171] includes zero, correctly identifying direct effect. Indirect Effect abab estimated 0.406 (true value 0.35). interval [0.215, 0.597] cross zero, indicating significant mediation. sum direct indirect effects (c+abc + ab). tells us 1-unit increase XX leads total increase roughly 0.349 YY. title: Multilevel SEM
text: use Demo.twolevel dataset included R package lavaan, make easier follow, interpret variables within educational context. data contains following information: Clusters (cluster): 200 different schools. Observations: 2500 students nested within schools. Outcomes (y1, y2, y3): Three distinct survey items measuring “Academic Performance.” Within-level Predictors (x1, x2, x3): Student-specific factors Study Hours, Sleep Hours, Attendance. -level Predictors (w1, w2): School-level factors Teacher Experience, School Budget. model, assume two latent variables: fw measuring individual students aptitude (.e. student ability) fb measuring school quality, .e. shared variance performance attributable school environment. envisaged two-level SEM can visualized follows:
code: graph LR      %% --- Level 1: Within (Student) ---     subgraph L1 [Level 1: Within-Student]         direction LR         x1[x1] & x2[x2] & x3[x3] --> fw((fw))         fw --> y1_w[y1] & y2_w[y2] & y3_w[y3]     end      %% --- Separator (Invisible edge to force stacking if needed) ---     L1 ~~~ L2      %% --- Level 2: Between (School) ---     subgraph L2 [Level 2: Between-School]         direction LR         w1[w1] & w2[w2] --> fb((fb))         fb --> y1_b[y1] & y2_b[y2] & y3_b[y3]     end          %% --- Styling ---     classDef latent fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,shape:circle;     classDef observed fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,shape:rect;      class fw,fb latent;     class x1,x2,x3,w1,w2,y1_b,y2_b,y3_b,y1_w,y2_w,y3_w observed;

title: Multilevel SEM
text: First, load INLAvaan dataset.

title: Multilevel SEM
text: INLAvaan (following lavaan syntax), specify model level using level: <block> keywords. example, Level 1: define latent variable fw (Student Ability) regress individual predictors (x). Level 2: define latent variable fb (School Quality) regress school-level predictors (w). use asem() function (Approximate SEM) fit model. Crucially, must specify cluster argument identify grouping variable.
code: mod <- "   level: 1       # Measurement model (Within-student)       fw =~ y1 + y2 + y3       # Structural model: Individual predictors       fw ~ x1 + x2 + x3    level: 2       # Measurement model (Between-school)       fb =~ y1 + y2 + y3       # Structural model: School-level predictors       fb ~ w1 + w2 " fit <- asem(mod, data = Demo.twolevel, cluster = "cluster") #> ℹ Finding posterior mode. #> ✔ Finding posterior mode. title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: fitting Bayesian SEM INLAvaan, often want draw samples model —either using posterior prior parameter values. sampling() function exactly : propagates parameter draws full generative chain 𝛉⏟parameters→𝛈⏟latent variables→𝐲*⏟observed variables \underbrace{\boldsymbol\theta}_{\text{parameters}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\boldsymbol\eta}_{\text{latent variables}} \;\longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\mathbf{y}^*}_{\text{observed variables}} producing samples tied individual observation. distinct predict(), returns individual-specific factor scores 𝛈∣𝐲,𝛉\boldsymbol\eta \mid \mathbf{y}, \boldsymbol\theta. Typical use cases include posterior predictive checks (PPCs) prior predictive checks.

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: Let 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} (s=1,…,Ss = 1, \dots, S) denote one parameter draw. draw SEM matrices 𝚲\boldsymbol\Lambda, 𝚿\boldsymbol\Psi, 𝐁\mathbf{B}, 𝛂\boldsymbol\alpha, 𝛎\boldsymbol\nu, 𝚯\boldsymbol\Theta constructed. generative chain : 1. Latent variables. 𝛈(s)∼𝒩((𝐈−𝐁)−1𝛂,𝚽),𝚽=(𝐈−𝐁)−1𝚿[(𝐈−𝐁)−1]′. \boldsymbol\eta^{(s)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(   (\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}\boldsymbol\alpha,\;   \boldsymbol\Phi \bigr), \qquad \boldsymbol\Phi = (\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}\boldsymbol\Psi\,   [(\mathbf{} - \mathbf{B})^{-1}]'. 2. Observed variables. 𝐲*(s)∼𝒩(𝚲𝛈(s)+𝛎,𝚯). \mathbf{y}^{*(s)} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\bigl(   \boldsymbol\Lambda\,\boldsymbol\eta^{(s)} + \boldsymbol\nu,\;   \boldsymbol\Theta \bigr). prior = FALSE (default), 𝛉(s)\boldsymbol\theta^{(s)} comes posterior; prior = TRUE, parameter drawn independently prior.

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: default type "lavaan" returns S×pS \times p matrix lavaan-side (constrained) parameter draws:
code: dat <- lavaan::HolzingerSwineford1939 mod <- "   visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3   textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6   speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9 " fit <- acfa(mod, dat, verbose = FALSE) theta_post <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 2000) dim(theta_post) #> [1] 2000   21 head(theta_post[, 1:4]) #>      visual=~x2 visual=~x3 textual=~x5 textual=~x6 #> [1,]  0.6216553  0.9058102    1.124324   0.9962591 #> [2,]  0.5512186  0.8048597    1.213441   0.9163449 #> [3,]  0.5589617  0.7005065    0.980363   0.9248275 #> [4,]  0.5567215  0.7637923    1.141509   1.0359674 #> [5,]  0.5948079  1.0228482    1.073171   0.8665535 #> [6,]  0.3633649  0.7067464    1.089029   0.8697055 eta  <- sampling(fit, type = "latent",   nsamp = 1000) ystar <- sampling(fit, type = "observed", nsamp = 1000) dim(eta)    # 1000 x 3 (one per latent factor) #> [1] 1000    3 dim(ystar)  # 1000 x 9 (one per observed indicator) #> [1] 1000    9 all_samps <- sampling(fit, type = "all", nsamp = 1000) names(all_samps) #> [1] "lavaan"   "theta"    "latent"   "observed" "implied"

what: Comparing samples to the fitted marginals
title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: INLAvaan approximates marginal posterior skew-normal density. can overlay sampling() histogram (drawn via copula method) top fitted density curve stored pdf_data verify agree. Figure 1: Copula samples (histogram) versus fitted skew-normal marginal (red curve) free parameters.
code: # 1. Draw copula samples samp_cop <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 1000, samp_copula = TRUE)  # 2. Retrieve the fitted skew-normal densities int <- fit@external$inlavaan_internal pdf_data <- int$pdf_data  # 3. Build a long data frame for ggplot par_names <- colnames(samp_cop) hist_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_cop)),   value = as.vector(samp_cop) ) hist_df$param <- factor(hist_df$param, levels = par_names)  curve_df <- do.call(rbind, Map(function(nm, df) {   data.frame(param = nm, x = df$x, y = df$y) }, par_names, pdf_data[par_names])) curve_df$param <- factor(curve_df$param, levels = par_names)  # 4. Plot ggplot(hist_df, aes(x = value)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50,                  fill = "grey40", alpha = 0.5) +   geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = y),             colour = "#00A6AA", linewidth = 0.8) +   facet_wrap(~param, scales = "free") +   labs(x = "Parameter value", y = "Density",        title = "Copula samples vs. fitted skew-normal posterior") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: default, sampling() uses copula method, respects skew-normal marginals. Setting samp_copula = FALSE uses multivariate Gaussian (Laplace) approximation instead. difference visible parameters asymmetric posteriors, variance components. Figure 2: Comparison marginal histograms copula sampling (blue) Gaussian sampling (orange) parameters. symmetric posteriors (e.g., factor loadings), two methods nearly identical. positively-skewed posteriors (e.g., residual variances), copula method captures asymmetry Gaussian approximation symmetric construction.
code: samp_gauss <- sampling(fit, type = "lavaan", nsamp = 5000, samp_copula = FALSE)  cop_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_cop)),   value = as.vector(samp_cop),   method = "Copula" ) gauss_df <- data.frame(   param = rep(par_names, each = nrow(samp_gauss)),   value = as.vector(samp_gauss),   method = "Gaussian" ) both_df <- rbind(cop_df, gauss_df) both_df$param <- factor(both_df$param, levels = par_names) both_df$method <- factor(both_df$method, levels = c("Copula", "Gaussian"))  ggplot(both_df, aes(x = value, fill = method)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50,                  alpha = 0.45, position = "identity") +   facet_wrap(~param, scales = "free") +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(Copula = "#00A6AA", Gaussian = "#F18F00")) +   labs(x = "Parameter value", y = "Density", fill = "Method",        title = "Copula vs. Gaussian posterior sampling") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +   theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Sampling from the Generative Model
text: Setting prior = TRUE draws parameters prior distribution (independently, ignoring data) propagates generative model. useful checking whether priors imply sensible ranges observed data. Figure 3: Model-implied distribution x1 prior (orange) posterior (blue).
code: y_prior <- sampling(fit, type = "observed", nsamp = 10000, prior = TRUE) #> Prior sampling: 6253 of 16253 draws (38.5%) rejected (non-PD model-implied #> covariance). y_post  <- sampling(fit, type = "observed", nsamp = 10000, prior = FALSE) df_pp <- data.frame(   x1 = c(y_prior[, "x1"], y_post[, "x1"]),   source = rep(c("Prior", "Posterior"), each = 2000) )  ggplot(df_pp, aes(x = x1, fill = source)) +   geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50,                  alpha = 0.5, position = "identity") +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(Prior = "#F18F00", Posterior = "#00A6AA")) +   labs(x = "x1", y = "Density", fill = NULL,        title = "Prior vs. posterior predictive distribution of x1") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(legend.position = "top")

title: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis
text: Efficient approximate Bayesian inference Structural Equation Models. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods remain gold standard exact Bayesian inference, can prohibitively slow iterative model development. INLAvaan offers rapid alternative latent variable analysis, delivering Bayesian results (near) speed frequentist estimators. achieves custom, ground-implementation Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA), engineered specifically lavaan modelling framework.

title: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis
text: INLAvaan designed fit seamlessly existing workflow. familiar (b)lavaan syntax, can begin using INLAvaan immediately. first impression package, consider canonical example SEM applied Industrialisation Political Democracy data set Bollen (1989)1:
code: library(INLAvaan) mod_poldem <- "   # Latent variable definitions      ind60 =~ x1 + x2 + x3      dem60 =~ y1 + y2 + y3      dem65 =~ y5 + y6 + y7 + y8    # Latent regressions     dem60 ~ ind60     dem65 ~ ind60 + dem60    # Residual correlations     y1 ~~ y5     y2 ~~ y4 + y6     y3 ~~ y7     y4 ~~ y8     y6 ~~ y8      # Fixed loading     dem60 =~ 1.5*y4      # Custom priors on latent variances     ind60 ~~ prior('gamma(1, 1)')*ind60     dem60 ~~ prior('gamma(2, 1)')*dem60     dem65 ~~ prior('gamma(1,.5)')*dem65 " utils::data("PoliticalDemocracy", package = "lavaan")  fit <- asem(model = mod_poldem, data = PoliticalDemocracy) #> ℹ Finding posterior mode. #> ✔ Finding posterior mode. what: INLAvaan: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis — INLAvaan-package
title: INLAvaan: Approximate Bayesian Latent Variable Analysis — INLAvaan-package
text: Implements approximate Bayesian inference Structural Equation Models (SEM) using custom adaptation Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (Rue et al., 2009) doi:10.1111/j.1467-9868.2008.00700.x  described Jamil Rue (2026a) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2603.25690 . Provides computationally efficient alternative Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) Bayesian estimation, allowing users fit latent variable models using 'lavaan' syntax. See also companion paper implementation workflows, Jamil Rue (2026b) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2604.00671 .

title: Fit an Approximate Bayesian Confirmatory Factor Analysis Model — acfa
text: model description user-specified model. Typically, model     described using lavaan model syntax. See     model.syntax information. Alternatively,     parameter table (eg. output lavParTable() function) also     accepted. data optional data frame containing observed variables used     model. variables declared ordered factors, lavaan     treat ordinal variables. dp Default prior distributions different types model parameters; named character vector returned priors_for(). test Character indicating post-estimation quantities compute. Defaults "standard": posterior fit indices (PPP DIC), plus – models supported casewise machinery fitted mean structure – WAIC (reusing fit's posterior draws, nsamp >= 100) full leave-one-cross-validation whenever predicted serial cost within 10-second budget; stored fit (see loo() waic()). "none" skips . Include "loo" (e.g. test = c("standard", "loo"), test = "loo" alone) force full LOO regardless budget. vb_correction Logical indicating whether apply variational Bayes correction posterior mean vector estimates. Defaults TRUE. marginal_method method approximating marginal posterior distributions. Options include "skewnorm" (skew-normal), "asymgaus" (two-piece asymmetric Gaussian), "marggaus" (marginalising Laplace approximation), "sampling" (sampling joint Laplace approximation). marginal_correction type correction use fitting skew-normal two-piece Gaussian marginals. "hessian" computes full "shortcut" (default) computes diagonals via central differences (full z-trace plus Schur complement correction), "shortcut_fd" formula using forward differences (roughly half cost, less accurate), "hessian" computes full Hessian-based correction (slow), "none" (FALSE) applies correction. nsamp number samples draw sampling-based approaches (including posterior sampling model fit indices). samp_copula Logical. TRUE (default), posterior samples drawn using copula method fitted marginals (e.g. skew-normal asymmetric Gaussian), NORTA correlation adjustment. FALSE, samples drawn Gaussian (Laplace) approximation. re sn_fit_ngrid Number grid points lay per dimension fitting skew-normal marginals. finer grid gives better fit cost joint-log-posterior evaluations. title: Compare Bayesian Models Fitted with INLAvaan — compare
text: first argument x serves baseline (null) model. models (including baseline) appear comparison table. baseline also passed fitMeasures() incremental fit indices (BCFI, BTLI, BNFI) requested via fit.measures. default table always includes: npar: Number free parameters. Marg.Loglik: Approximated marginal log-likelihood. logBF: Natural-log Bayes factor relative best model. DIC / pD: Deviance Information Criterion effective number parameters (test != "none" used fitting). Set fit.measures character vector measure names (anything returned fitMeasures()) append extra columns. Use fit.measures = "" include every available measure. Set loo = TRUE compare models leave-one-cross-validation (see loo()). appends ELPD / SE (second-order Taylor expected log predictive density standard error), p_loo, , best-ELPD model, difference elpd_diff paired standard error se_diff computed pointwise contributions (appropriate uncertainty nested -data comparisons). table sorted descending ELPD. models must fitted data matching units, must share score flavour (see loo()): mixing fits modelled covariates (fixed.x = FALSE, joint scores) fixed covariates (fixed.x = TRUE, conditional scores) refused. Joint scores additionally require identical variable sets across models, conditional scores require matching outcome variables – covariate sets may differ, covariate-selection setting. Stored LOO results (test = "loo" add_loo()) reused.

title: Convergence and Approximation Diagnostics for INLAvaan Models — diagnostics
text: type = "global", named numeric vector (class "diagnostics.INLAvaan"). type = "param", data frame (class c("diagnostics.INLAvaan.param", "data.frame")).

title: Convergence and Approximation Diagnostics for INLAvaan Models — diagnostics
text: Global diagnostics (type = "global"): npar Number free parameters. nsamp Number posterior samples drawn. converged 1 optimiser converged, 0 otherwise. iterations Number optimiser iterations. grad_inf L-infinity norm analytic gradient mode (max |grad|). ~0 convergence. grad_inf_rel Relative L-infinity norm analytic gradient (max |grad| / (|par| + 1e-6)). grad_l2 L2 (Euclidean) norm analytic gradient mode. hess_cond Condition number Hessian (precision matrix) computed \(\Sigma_\theta\). Large values indicate near-singularity. vb_kld_global Global KL divergence VB mean correction (NA VB correction applied). vb_applied 1 VB correction applied, 0 otherwise. kld_max Maximum per-parameter KL divergence VB correction. kld_mean Mean per-parameter KL divergence. nmad_max Maximum normalised max-absolute-deviation across marginals (skew-normal method ; NA otherwise). nmad_mean Mean NMAD across marginals. Per-parameter diagnostics (type = "param"): data frame columns: param Parameter name. grad Analytic gradient negative log-posterior mode. ~0 convergence. grad_num Numerical (finite-difference) gradient mode. agree grad; large discrepancies indicate bug analytic gradient. grad_diff Difference grad_num - grad: ~0. grad_abs Absolute analytic gradient. grad_rel Relative analytic gradient |grad| / (|par| + 1e-6). kld Per-parameter KL divergence VB correction. vb_shift VB correction shift (original scale). vb_shift_sigma VB shift units posterior SD. nmad Normalised max-absolute-deviation skew-normal fit (NA using skewnorm method).

title: Fit a skew normal distribution to log-density evaluations — fit_skew_normal
code: # Fit a SN curve to gamma log-density logdens <- function(x) dgamma(x, shape = 3, rate = 1, log = TRUE)  x_grid <- seq(0.1, 8, length.out = 21) y_log  <- sapply(x_grid, logdens) y_log  <- y_log - max(y_log)  # normalise to have maximum at zero  res <- fit_skew_normal(x_grid, y_log, temp = 10) unlist(res) #>           xi        omega        alpha         logC            k         rmse  #>  0.842445393  2.539226101  3.310505376  1.332422700 10.000000000  0.002109188  #>         nmad  #>  0.173298470   # Compare truth vs skew-normal approximation x_fine <- seq(0.1, 8, length.out = 200) y_true <- exp(logdens(x_fine)) y_sn   <- dsnorm(x_fine, xi = res$xi, omega = res$omega, alpha = res$alpha)  plot(x_fine, y_true, type = "n", xlab = "x", ylab = "Density", bty = "n") polygon(c(x_fine, rev(x_fine)), c(y_true, rep(0, length(x_fine))),         col = adjustcolor("#131516", 0.25), border = NA) lines(x_fine, y_sn, col = "#00A6AA", lwd = 2) legend("topright", legend = c("Truth", "SN Approx."),        fill = c(adjustcolor("#131516", 0.25), NA), border = NA,        col = c(NA, "#00A6AA"), lwd = c(NA, 2), lty = c(NA, 1),        bty = "n")

Alternatively,     parameter table (eg. output lavParTable() function) also     accepted. data optional data frame containing observed variables used     model. variables declared ordered factors, lavaan     treat ordinal variables. model.type Set model type: possible values     "cfa", "sem" "growth". may affect     starting values computed, may used alter terminology     used summary output, layout path diagrams     based fitted lavaan object. dp Default prior distributions different types model parameters; named character vector returned priors_for(). test Character indicating post-estimation quantities compute. Defaults "standard": posterior fit indices (PPP DIC), plus – models supported casewise machinery fitted mean structure – WAIC (reusing fit's posterior draws, nsamp >= 100) full leave-one-cross-validation whenever predicted serial cost within 10-second budget; stored fit (see loo() waic()). "none" skips . Include "loo" (e.g. test = c("standard", "loo"), test = "loo" alone) force full LOO regardless budget. vb_correction Logical indicating whether apply variational Bayes correction posterior mean vector estimates. Defaults TRUE. marginal_method method approximating marginal posterior distributions. Options include "skewnorm" (skew-normal), "asymgaus" (two-piece asymmetric Gaussian), "marggaus" (marginalising Laplace approximation), "sampling" (sampling joint Laplace approximation). marginal_correction type correction use fitting skew-normal two-piece Gaussian marginals. "hessian" computes full "shortcut" (default) computes diagonals via central differences (full z-trace plus Schur complement correction), "shortcut_fd" formula using forward differences (roughly half cost, less accurate), "hessian" computes full Hessian-based correction (slow), "none" (FALSE) applies correction. nsamp number samples draw sampling-based approaches (including posterior sampling model fit indices). samp_copula Logical. TRUE (default), posterior samples drawn using copula method fitted marginals (e.g. skew-normal asymmetric Gaussian), NORTA correlation adjustment. FALSE, samples drawn Gaussian (Laplace) approximation. re sn_fit_ngrid Number grid points lay per dimension fitting skew-normal marginals. finer grid gives better fit cost joint-log-posterior evaluations. Defaults 21. sn_fit_logthresh log-threshold fitting skew-normal. Points log-posterior drop threshold (relative maximum) excluded fit. Defaults -6. sn_fit_temp Temperature parameter fitting skew-normal. Defaults 1 (weights density values ). NA, temperature included additional optimisation parameter. sn_fit_sample Logical. TRUE (default), parametric skew-normal fitted posterior samples covariance defined parameters. FALSE, summarised using kernel density estimation instead. control list control parameters optimiser. verbose Logical indicating whether print progress messages. debug Logical indicating whether return debug information. add_priors Logical indicating whether include prior densities posterior computation. optim_method optimisation method use finding posterior mode. Options include "nlminb" (default), "ucminf", "optim" (BFGS). numerical_grad Logical indicating whether use numerical gradients optimisation. Defaults FALSE use analytical gradients. cores Integer NULL. Number cores parallel marginal fitting. NULL (default), serial execution used unless number free parameters exceeds 120, case parallelisation enabled automatically using available physical cores. Set 1L force serial execution. cores > 1, marginal fits distributed across cores using parallel::mclapply() (fork-based; parallelism Windows). ... Additional arguments passed lavaan::lavaan model fitting function.

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: Vector quantiles.

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: function implements high-performance approximation skew-normal quantile function based algorithm described Luu (2016). method uses domain decomposition strategy achieve high accuracy (\(< 10^{-7}\) relative error) without iterative numerical inversion. domain split two regions: Tail Regions: extreme probabilities \(\vert u \vert\) large, quantile approximated using Lambert W-function, \(W(z)\), solving \(z = \Phi(q)\) via asymptotic expansion: $$q \approx \sqrt{2 W\left(\frac{1}{2\pi (1-p)^2}\right)}$$ Central Region: main body distribution, function uses high-order Taylor expansion inverse error function around carefully selected expansion point $x_0$: $$\Phi^{-1}(p) \approx \sum_{k=0}^5 c_k (z - x_0)^k$$ approach significantly faster standard numerical inversion (e.g., uniroot) maintaining sufficient precision statistical applications.

title: Fast Approximation of Skew-Normal Quantile Function — qsnorm_fast
text: Luu, T. (2016). Fast accurate parallel computation quantile functions random number generation (Doctoral thesis). UCL (University College London). https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/1482128/

title: Widely Applicable Information Criterion for INLAvaan Models — waic
text: posterior draw \(\theta_s\) (drawn copula sampler used fit's posterior summaries) unit \(u\), log-likelihood \(\log p(y_u \mid \theta_s)\) evaluated; \(\mathrm{lpd}_u = \log \tfrac{1}{S}\sum_s p(y_u \mid \theta_s)\), \(p_{\mathrm{waic},u} = \mathrm{var}_s \log p(y_u \mid \theta_s)\), \(\mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{waic}} = \sum_u (\mathrm{lpd}_u - p_{\mathrm{waic},u})\) \(\mathrm{WAIC} = -2\, \mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{waic}}\). Unlike loo(), sampling-based estimate: results vary random draws, units \(p_{\mathrm{waic},u} > 0.4\) trigger reliability warning (also annotated printing). model restrictions loo() apply, flavour rule: fits fixed.x = TRUE scored conditionally exogenous covariates, fits fixed.x = FALSE jointly (see loo()). default test = "standard", inlavaan() computes WAIC fit time reusing posterior draws fit already produced (model supported, mean structure, nsamp >= 100), stores fit: waic(fit) returns stored result called default arguments, fitmeasures() reports waic, p_waic, se_waic part "" free. loo package attached masks generic, dispatch INLAvaan objects continues work.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.5

title: INLAvaan 0.2.5
text: INLAvaan now works current lavaan 0.6 series upcoming lavaan 0.7, renames many internal functions. lavaan internals INLAvaan relies now resolved package loads, whichever naming scheme available. lavaan (>= 0.6-19) now declared explicitly, package checked oldest supported, current CRAN, development versions lavaan CI. Fixed trapezoid rule used compare_mcmc() density normalisation, overlap, KL divergence computations. compare_mcmc() diagnostics() now robust NA values density diagnostic computations. dp argument inlavaan() friends now documented terms priors_for().

title: INLAvaan 0.2.4
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-03

title: INLAvaan 0.2.4
text: bfit_indices() computes per-sample Bayesian fit index vectors (BRMSEA, BCFI, BTLI, BNFI), summary() print() methods. Summary statistics also available via fitmeasures(). compare() compares two fitted models side side, reporting marginal log-likelihood, Bayes factors, DIC, optional fit measures fitmeasures(). diagnostics() computes global per-parameter convergence approximation-quality diagnostics fitted models. get_inlavaan_internal() now exported documented, providing access internal list stored fitted INLAvaan object. predict() generates predictions observed data missing data imputation, respecting multilevel structure present. sampling() draws posterior (prior) SEM generative model, returning parameter vectors, latent variables, observed variables. simulate() generates complete replicate datasets fitted model, useful simulation-based calibration posterior predictive checks. timing() extracts wall-clock timings individual computation stages fitted model.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.4
text: Cholesky factorisation precision matrix replaces raw solve() covariance log-determinant calculations. Copula sampling NORTA (NORmal Anything) correlation adjustment now default (samp_copula = TRUE), ensuring posterior samples correct skew-normal marginals correct Pearson correlations. Pre-computed Owen-scrambled Sobol sequences used default, fallback {qrng} larger sequences. QMC sample size now scales model dimension. Skew-normal fitting now runs parallel automatically number marginals exceeds 120, using available cores. Small optimisations skew-normal volume correction. acfa(), asem(), agrowth() gain vb_correction argument. ggplot2 now optional; plots fall back base R graphics installed. inlavaan() gains sn_fit_ngrid argument control number grid points per dimension fitting skew-normal marginals (default 21). inlavaan() now supports sn_fit_sample = TRUE defined parameters, fitting skew-normal approximation posterior marginals based drawn samples. plot() method gains improved visualisation options. priors_for() now supports [prec] scale qualifier variance parameters (theta, psi), placing prior precision scale automatic Jacobian adjustment. sampling() simulate() gain silent argument suppress informational messages. summary() now includes 25th 75th percentile columns. vcov() now returns covariance matrix lavaan-side parameters supports type argument choosing sample Laplace covariance.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.4
text: marginal_correction = "shortcut" longer produces incorrect volume corrections. qsnorm_fast() longer incorrectly handles sign symmetries.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.3
text: CRAN release: 2026-01-28 Improved axis scanning, skewness correction, VB mean correction routine. Bug fixes CRAN. Updated README example.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.2
text: CRAN release: 2026-01-27 hood, use lavaan’s MVN log-likelihood function compute single- multi-level log-likelihoods. Added support multi-level SEM models. Added support binary data using PML estimator lavaan. NOTE: Ordinal possible theory, package still lacks proper prior support thresholds. Added support missing = "ML" handle FIML missing data.

title: INLAvaan 0.2.1
text: Support lavaan 0.6-21. Implemented variational Bayes mean correction posterior marginals. Defined parameters now available, e.g. mediation analysis. Prepare CRAN release.

title: INLAvaan 0.2
text: INLAvaan rewritten ground specifically SEM models. new version call R-INLA directly, instead uses core approximation ideas fit SEM models efficiently. Features restricted normal likelihoods continuous observations now. Support models lavaan/blavaan can fit, including CFA, SEM, growth curve models. Support multigroup analysis. Added PPP DIC model fit indices. Added prior specification model parameters. Added support fixed values parameter constraints. Initial CRAN submission.

title: INLAvaan 0.1
text: Used rgeneric functionality R-INLA implement basic SEM framework.
